back: A join operation that combines tuples from two relations based on a general condition (θ can be =, <, >, ≤, ≥, ≠). It is equivalent to Cartesian Product followed by Selection.
A special case of Theta join where the join condition uses only equality (=) operator. It finds matching tuples based on common attribute values.
An equijoin that automatically matches tuples based on all attributes with the same name in both relations, removing the duplicate attribute from the result.
An extension of natural join that preserves tuples from one or both relations that do not have matching values, filling NULL values for missing attributes.

front: What are the three types of Outer Joins?
back: Left Outer Join (preserves all tuples from left relation), Right Outer Join (preserves all tuples from right relation), Full Outer Join (preserves all tuples from both relations).
A join that returns only tuples from one relation that match the join condition with the other relation, without including all attributes of the second relation.

front: What is the Division operation in relational algebra?
back: Given relations R(X, Y) and S(Y), R ÷ S returns tuples in X that are associated with every tuple in S. Used for 'for all' queries.
An operation that returns tuples common to both relations. Can be expressed as R ∩ S = R - (R - S) or R ∩ S = (R ∪ S) - (R - S) - (S - R).

front: What is a Natural Join notation in relational algebra?
back: R ⋈ S denotes natural join between relations R and S. It automatically joins on common attributes and removes duplicate columns.

front: Why is Division operation computationally expensive?
back: Division requires checking for each tuple in X whether it is associated with ALL tuples in S, making it expensive for large relations.

front: What is the difference between Inner Join and Outer Join?
back: Inner Join returns only tuples with matching values in both relations. Outer Join also includes non-matching tuples from one or both relations.

front: How is Right Outer Join implemented using Left Outer Join?
back: R ⟕ S = S ⟖ R (swap relations and use left outer join) or R ⟖ S = S ⟕ R. The order of relations matters in outer joins.
A join operation where a relation is joined with itself, requiring tuple variable notation (e.g., R AS X, R AS Y) to distinguish between copies.

front: What is the purpose of the Rename operation (ρ) in complex queries?
back: Rename is used to assign new names to relations or attributes, essential for self-joins and to eliminate ambiguity in attribute names.
R ∩ S = R - (R - S) = S - (S - R). This uses set difference to find common tuples.
